    unordered_set<string> subs;

So the code would be:

class Solution {
public:
    bool isSubstringPresent(string s) {
        string rev_s(s.rbegin(), s.rend());
        unordered_set<string> subs;
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length()-1; ++i) {
            subs.insert(s.substr(i, 2));
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < rev_s.length()-1; ++i) {
            if (subs.count(rev_s.substr(i, 2))) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    bool isSubstringPresent(string s) {
        string rev_s(s.rbegin(), s.rend());
        unordered_set<string> subs;
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length()-1; ++i) {
            subs.insert(s.substr(i, 2));
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < rev_s.length()-1; ++i) {
            if (subs.count(rev_s.substr(i, 2))) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution collects all length-2 substrings of the original string in a set, then checks if any of these substrings exist in the reversed string. This ensures O(n) time and space complexity. The key strength is simplicity and linear efficiency, but the approach uses extra space for the set.
